title: Time Allocation

content.statement: Your life = how you allocate time.
content.question: Does your calendar match your stated priorities this week?
sop: Track every hour for one full week, categorized by activity.
Rate each activity's Priority Weight 1-3 (low/medium/high) based on your actual stated goals (see calculation, page 4)
Multiply Time spent by Priority Weight for each activity and sum the totals (see calculation, page 4)
Identify the highest-time, lowest-priority-weight activities, these are your biggest misalignments.
Rebuild next week's calendar to shift hours toward your highest-weighted priorities.
questions: Does your calendar match your stated priorities this week?
If a stranger only saw your calendar, what would they guess your top priority actually is?
How many hours this week went to something you'd rate below a 5 out of 10 in importance?
content.equation_example: Task A: Time 3 hours times Priority Weight 3 equals 9. Task B: Time 5 hours times Priority Weight 1 equals 5. Total equals 14 priority-weighted hours. Higher means your hours are concentrated on what actually matters to you. Lower means you're logging hours that don't move anything important, no matter how busy they feel.
content.closing_statement: A full calendar isn't the same as a good one.

caption:
Principle Nº 154: Time Allocation

Your life = how you allocate time.

Ask yourself: Does your calendar match your stated priorities this week?
